steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage contrasts private (republican-era) and public (imperial-era) Roman libraries, then pivots with 'however' to Fernandes's thesis: the two traditions aren't as distinct as they seem because both served as extensions of personal patronage. The correct answer must capture this central similarity claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'republican access only': Focuses solely on who could use republican-era libraries; ignores the patronage similarity that is the passage's whole point. ✗
  • Choice B — 'value judgment': Makes a claim about which type was more valuable — a judgment the passage never makes. ✗
  • Choice C — 'both traditions shared a patronage function': Directly reflects Fernandes's thesis that private and public Roman libraries were alike in serving as tools of personal patronage. ✓
  • Choice D — 'disappearance of libraries': Introduces the end of Roman libraries, a topic the passage never addresses. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
